# AgentOverflow

> Experiment framework testing whether AI coding agents benefit from a shared code solution registry. Three agents (Alpha: baseline, Beta: cold start + post, Gamma: warm cache + pull) compete on identical tasks. Results show the registry only helps when tasks take 10+ turns from scratch — for most tasks, the model's own knowledge is faster than retrieving cached solutions.

## Docs

- [README](README.md): Setup, CLI usage, experiment results, how to test your own tasks
- [Experiment Report](EXPERIMENT_REPORT.md): Full scientific write-up with raw data from 36 runs on hard tasks
- [Agent Guide](AGENTS.md): Build steps, project structure, key files for AI agents

## CLI

```
npx agentoverflow add <task.md>    Register tasks (markdown files with your prompt)
npx agentoverflow run              Run experiment on all registered tasks
npx agentoverflow run --hard       Run built-in hard benchmark tasks
npx agentoverflow list             Show registered tasks
npx agentoverflow dashboard        Open results dashboard
npx agentoverflow init             Install dependencies
```

## Architecture

- MCP Server (`agentstack/`): SQLite-backed registry with 3 tools — search, pull, post
- Experiment Runner (`agentstack-dashboard/experiments/runner.ts`): Spawns claude CLI for 3-agent A/B/C comparison
- Dashboard (`agentstack-dashboard/`): React + Express UI for viewing results
- Tasks (`tasks/`): Markdown files containing prompts — auto-loaded by runner

## Key Findings

- Model completes "hard" tasks (DAG scheduler, event sourcing, markdown compiler) in 4-8 turns, not the expected 14-18
- Registry workflow has an irreducible floor of ~8 turns (search + pull + write + run + report)
- Registry only outperforms baseline when from-scratch build exceeds ~10 turns (25% of test cases)
- Registry acts as a variance reducer (consistent 7-10 turns) not an accelerator
